there are eleven islands; their shores bathed by the warm waters of the Japanese current, or Kuro Shiwo, and their sombre peaks and verdant valleys tempered by the fresh Westerly and the California sun.
From these and other weather factors they gain a climate that is hard to equal, having the life of the Mediterranean's
without its chill winds and the tropic beauty of

without its heat. Actually, they are the tops of mountains rising out of the sea, transformed from barren peaks by a combination of meteorological conditions into gardens of rare beauty and charm. For every man who loves the outdoors the

Their blue and crystal-clear waters are the home of that bucking bronco of the sea, the leaping tuna, and all of his swaggering cousins, the albacore, the yellowtail, the bonito. Anglers come from all over the world to try their skill against these grand fighters, as well as the marlin and broadbill swordfish, the white sea bass, the blanquillo and a host of smaller game fishes.

They are the yachtsman's paradise, for it is hard to think of another place on earth where a sailor could find such perfect cruising grounds, with summer weather and fair winds the year around and scores of beautiful little harbors where the yacht on leisurely cruise may anchor in safety. As for the thrill of cruising races, ask any Southland yachtsman who has fought his way down the west shore of
during a night of contrary currents and constantly shifting breezes, finally to round
and then go boiling home to
They have equal thrill for the hunter who has clambered rough canyons and over the needle crags of
in search of wild goats, or for his brother who in the dead of night has crawled for two miles on his belly to stalk the elusive white geese feeding on the tableland on
who have hunted wild boars on
have enjoyed real danger and excitement and the man who likes upland game bird shooting can find his fill of doves and quail in the verdant slopes and wooded canyons of
are divided into three distinct groups. Northernmost are the
, lying about twenty miles offshore and ranging in an east-west line from

via steamers of the Wrigley fleet alone, in addition to the thousands who traveled to

, spoke a dialect called Netela, but among

to be physically and mentally superior to the Indians of the mainland and expressed admiration of their ability in building canoes and the dexterity with which they handled these craft, as well as their use of steatite for making mortars. These mortars, made at the steatite quarries at
are believed to have taken across
and sold to the mainland natives as the real beginning of the port's sea-borne trade.
Excerpts from the diary of the Costanso expedition, which came up the

They are of good figure and aspect, men and women; very much given to painting and staining with red ochre their faces and bodies. They use great headdresses of feathers, and some banderillas which they bind up amid their hair, with various trinkets and beads of coral of various colors. . . . The men work handsome trays of wood, with firm inlays or coral or of bone; and some vases of much capacity, closing at the mouth, which appear to be made with a lathe—and with this machine they would not come out better-hollowed nor of more perfect form.

In their houses, the married couples have their separate beds on platforms elevated from the ground. Their mattresses are some simple petates or mats of rushes, and their pillows are of the same petates rolled up at the head. All of these beds are hung about with like mats, which serve for decency and protect from the cold.

is surpassing in the construction of their launches made of pine planking. They are from twenty-two to twenty-seven and one-half feet in length, including their rake, and of four feet beam . . . They handle the boats With equal cleverness, and three or four men go out to the open sea to fish in them, as they have capacity to carry eight or ten. They use long oars with two blades, and row with unspeakable lightness and velocity.
They know all the arts of fishing, and fish abound along their coasts, as has been said of

a paradise. The waters abounded then, as now, in marine life of every form, fish, seals, whales, sharks, crayfish, abalones, mussels. Vast heaps of these shells and skeletons still found at town sites attest to the fact that
within the last century is one of the mysteries of
the Lost Woman of San Nicolas
, the great sportsman who spent some thirty years exploring and studying the
One cannot look upon the human remains, the skulls and skeletons, without wondering where
went, or what accomplished their extinction, as within a century and a half
According to some, they were killed off by the Russians, Americans, or
yearly after sea otters, with which
abounded in the early part of the nineteenth century. These people, according to tradition, paid no attention to
, shot them down, and robbed them on a freebooter plan for mere amusement.
"To me a more plausible explanation of the disappearance of
induced them to give up their island life and settle about
, to be within easy reach of their teachings; and while many were killed in war, swept away by disease or by the invaders, doubtless
went to the various missions, where they soon lost their identity."
, lying some twenty miles southwest of

, is the weird fragment of some huge ancient volcano and its eighteen miles of lava flows, cactus-filled canyons, coast line caves and vast sand dunes present a remarkable spectacle of desolation, as compared to the grassy grazing plains of the middle island. Its windward coast is largely low and flat, with several bays and anchorages, but the eastern shore—apparently the inner rim of a giant volcano—presents nothing but towering cliffs of lava, in such grewsome, desolate expanse that one recalls the illustrations of Dante's Inferno.
and has long been inhabited by herders on the sheep ranch operated by

, and several coves shelter shacks of fishermen. Its highest peak rises to only 1960 feet, yet a great portion of it is so mountainous that it affords ideal country for the thousands of wild goats still hunted there about its needle-like lava crags and abysmal canyons.
offers a fair example of the unusual bird life of all the

. Its crags are the home of the American bald eagle and on the broad plains of the middle island are found valley quail, the mourning [morning] dove, humming birds, the savanna sparrow, rock wren, the house finch, lark, 'pewee, the California shrike and most of the coast's warblers. Ravens abound on

and about its sea-torn cliffs live the American osprey, the white-throated swift and the belted kingfisher. Sea birds making the various islands their winter home include the royal tern, the black-footed albatross, pelicans,cormorants, grebes, loons and auklets and the many species of shore waders. At certain seasons ducks and geese are found about the more verdant islands of
, the other two islands of the
. Santa Barbara's twin peaks rise out of
is some thirty farther offshore on almost the same bearing. A light has been estabilshed on little

, but it is uninhabited, save for large rookeries of cormorants, guillemots, pelicans, auklets and the upland birds of the other islands. Almost surrounded by huge kelp beds, Santa Barbara Island waters are fine fishing grounds but they are little frequented by amateur anglers because of the island's isolated position.
, inhabited only by the lonely herders tending the sheep kept there by Messrs.
, is the most desolate, wind-swept waste of the entire

last remnants of the dying tribe of Indians

never returned and the woman's isolation became a matter of years. Finally, after twenty years,
the padres of the Mission Santa Barbara
in search of her. On two trips he combed
from end to end without success and on the third trip he took along a
found her hut, made of whalebones and a brush roof, and later that day he and
sighted her, watching them from the opposite side of a hill. By the time they reached the spot, however, she had disappeared. From Brown's narrative:
, I said, 'There's nothing here, let's go back.' There was a basket and some feathers. She catched shags and had a coat made without sleeves, nicely covered with sealskin. I said to
, 'You go to the hill and scatter the feathers and things in the basket, and if she is still alive she will find them.' The same day we found them all gathered up and put in the basket."
resumed the search the next morning and soon caught sight of her toiling up the hill toward the whalebone hut, carrying something heavy in her arms. He describes her capture:

"She had a brush fence, about two feet high, to break the wind, and right in front of me she sat facing me. The sun was coming in her face. She was skinning a seal before I came up to her. Thinking she might run I stepped round her, and she bowed as if she knew me before, and when
" and by that name she was known. She was estimated to have been about forty-five years old when found. All efforts to find
who understood her dialect were fruitless and after six weeks at
she became ill and died—taking with her the enigma of the disappearance of the

San Nicolas' eight miles of lava flows, shifting sand dunes, its 900-foot fog-shrouded peak, its deep cut canyons, its bleak cliffs and the crashing seas breaking over its rocky shores make it just about one of the most desolate places on earth. Yet it was one of the most populous of all

and it is still a great treasure ground for archeologists and souvenir hunters. It is the site of one of the largest aboriginal kitchen middens in the world and here may still be found skulls, pipes, beads, ollas, flint spear heads and other traces of
is literally being blown into the sea and while the northwester is howling with great force across its desolate expanse one can stand on
, where at that same moment an ideal summer climate obtains.
Sailing up channel, the first of the
, "the everchanging," in reality three islands of grotesque shape.

is an island in the last stages of its existence, succumbing to the ravages of wind and sea, and its shores are therefore formed of myriad caves and arches, where the sea rushes in and out in its never-ceasing effort. The largest of Anacapa's three islets is a peak rising 980 feet above the sea,
, in fact, having the appearance of a mountain on edge rising out of the sea.
Although less than a hundred miles separate the
a striking change in climate is noted. The westerly wind blows with more gusto down the
and its waters seem to be the camp ground of the grim fogs which often envelop the Anacapa Passage.
islands, next in order up channel, on the other hand enjoy a salubrious climate, which is like that of
, tempered by a fresher, cooler west wind.
in size and topographical make-up, are surpassed by none of the
in wild, verdant beauty. Santa Cruz's shores are lined with scores of little coves and bays, there being yachtsmen and fishermen who will tell you that
—that perfect little gem of a harbor midway up the south shore—is the prettiest spot on earth.
is world-famous for its great multi-colored shore line caves. Several like the
, have sea entrances and open up into cavern-canopied lakes under the mountains, while
, near the east end, has both shore and sea entrances.
is owned by the heirs of
, a Frenchman who a half-century ago established on its interior valley a beautiful rancho that is still a faithful reproduction of the vineyard country of
. Sheep are also raised on the
, while the island's tree-filled valleys and grassy slopes abound with California valley quail and doves.
in size and natural beauty and contains one of the finest cattle ranches in
. It is the wild boar hunters' favorite territory and is a winter rendezvous for huge flocks of white geese, which feed on the upland grazing grounds. Like the other
is mountainous and cut by deep canyons. Its highest peak,
, rises to a height of 1565 feet.
, members of the pioneer California family, gained title to
in 1834, from whose hands it later passes to the
of turbulent waters and dengerous currents, lies

is first seen as twin peaks rising out of the sea. It is government-owned. Treeless and swept continually by shifting dunes and storms of sand driven by the strong northwester, its shores are for the most part bold and rocky.
on the north shore, is the only harbor of value and even it is filled with kelp and shoals.
are believed to lie, for it was there he died on January 3, 1543, after suffering intensely for two months from the arm he had broken while at
in October. Dying in those lonely, stormy waters far from home, Cabrillo's valor and courage stayed with him to the last, for
, his pilot, says in describing his passage:
"He charged them much, at the time of his death, that they would not give up the discovery, as far as possible, of all that coast."
No monument marks his grave, but beneath the shifting sands of
did more than all others to increase Spain's kingdom in the
, yet the gallant efforts of these
are commemorated by not a single prominent landmark along the coast that they were first to find.
